nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes and reef flats; found across a range of reef environments from turbid inshore reefs to clear-water outer reef zones

decorPreferences: open placement with adequate spacing from neighbors
avoid direct proximity to other corals due to sweeper tentacles

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental targeted feeding of meaty foods such as Mysis shrimp or brine shrimp is generally beneficial once or twice per week, preferably in the evening when sweeper tentacles are extended. Micro-plankton and other fine particulate foods are also accepted.

goodWith: other corals placed well outside sweeper tentacle range
avoidWith: neighboring corals within 15–20 cm (sweeper tentacle range)
other aggressive LPS corals placed in direct competition

imageCandidates.notes: Favites pentagona, photo by Josuevg. 1853x1991. Whole aquarium colony, sharp, vivid purple walls with fluorescent mint-green valleys clearly showing cerioid shared-wall structure - the classic hobby look.

Favites sp. under actinic/blue reef lighting, photo by Robert Howie. 7360x4912. Neon purple corallite walls with fluorescent green valleys - the buy-it-now reef look; partial-colony macro so kept as extra.
Favites sp., photo by Robert Howie. 7360x4912. Vivid red corallite walls with neon-green valleys, sharp; a different aquarium color morph.
Favites sp. with polyps/tentacles extended, photo by Robert Howie. 5370x4234. Red-orange walls, green valleys, sweeper tentacles visible - illustrates the aggression trait noted in care.
Favites abdita (Honeycomb Coral) in situ, photo by Bernard DUPONT. 4416x2649. Sharp, brown corallites with bright neon-green centers; textbook cerioid honeycomb pattern.
Favites abdita, photo by Stickpen. 2596x2626. Whole domed colony in aquarium showing the massive mound growth form with pale blue-green polyp centers.
Favites flexuosa, photo by Nick Hobgood. 1680x1260. Sharp, dramatic bright-green tentacles extended over purple-brown colony.
Favites complanata (probable) in situ, photo by Philippe Bourjon. 1200x932. Clean whole colony, sharp, cream/tan cerioid structure.
Favites pentagona in situ on natural reef, photo by Philippe Bourjon. 1200x930. Sharp, brown colony with green polyp dots - natural-habitat reference.
Favites rotundata in situ, photo by Debra Baker (via GBIF). 1536x2048. Whole yellow-olive domed colony with rounded corallites; adds a distinct genus species.

summary: Favites is a genus of large polyp stony (LPS) brain corals found across the Indo-Pacific. Generally considered beginner-friendly under moderate light and flow, Favites is also widely regarded as one of the more aggressive corals in the hobby, extending long sweeper tentacles after dark that can sting neighboring corals.
careNotes:
Favites is a genus of approximately 20 species of stony corals in the family Merulinidae. They grow in a solid, mound-like form — referred to in coral biology as a massive growth form — with a distinctive cerioid structure, meaning adjacent polyps share their walls rather than each sitting in a separate cup. This shared-wall arrangement sets Favites apart from the closely related genus Favia, whose polyps each have their own individual walls. The distinction is subtle, and many specimens in the aquarium trade are sold interchangeably between the two genera; husbandry requirements are essentially identical.

Key care notes:
• Lighting: Generally advised to keep under moderate light, approximately 75–200 PAR. A target range of 100–150 PAR is commonly recommended as optimal, with gradual acclimation possible up to 200 PAR over time. Sudden exposure to high-intensity light can cause bleaching.
• Flow: Moderate, indirect flow is typically recommended. Direct laminar currents tend to interfere with feeding and can irritate tissue; a gentle, turbulent flow pattern is preferred.
• Placement: Middle-to-lower rockwork is the common recommendation, where light intensity is moderate. Crucially, Favites tend to extend sweeper tentacles at night that have been reported to reach 7–10 cm or more beyond the colony base — all neighboring corals should be positioned well outside this range.
• Aggression: Favites is widely regarded as among the more aggressive LPS corals in the hobby. Night-time sweeper tentacles can sting and damage neighboring corals. Plan layout generously; spacing that seems adequate during the day may prove insufficient once tentacles extend after lights-off.
• Feeding: While zooxanthellae (the symbiotic algae living within the coral tissue) provide the majority of their energy needs through photosynthesis, Favites benefit from supplemental feeding. Targeted delivery of meaty foods such as Mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, or fine coral foods once or twice per week in the evening — when polyps are open and tentacles are visible — has been reported to support growth and coloration.
• Water chemistry: Standard LPS reef parameters are generally recommended: calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, alkalinity 8–11 dKH. Favites tends to be hardier than small polyp stony (SPS) corals and tolerates moderate nitrate and phosphate levels better than Acropora.
• Aquaculture: Many Favites frags and colonies are now aquacultured, and captive-grown specimens are available from specialty retailers and hobbyist propagation programs.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. Massive brain coral colonies can be fragged by cutting through the skeleton with a tile saw or bandsaw; individual corallites or small sections readily heal and encrust when mounted on frag plugs. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not routinely practiced in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page with care parameters: temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, lighting moderate, flow medium, care level easy, feeding twice per week.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Favites Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Care guide: temperature 77–84°F (25–28.9°C), pH 8.0–8.4, kH 8–9, calcium 400–450 ppm, lighting 100–150 PAR (max 200 PAR with acclimation), moderate indirect flow, care level beginner. Temperature upper bound disagreement with LiveAquaria (78°F vs 84°F) noted; averaged range of 24–27°C adopted.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Favites (taxonomy and biology)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Merulinidae, Order: Scleractinia), native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea through Western Pacific including Japan, Line Islands, Tuamotu Islands), species list (~20 spp.), and cerioid shared-wall distinction from Favia. Not used for husbandry parameters.
